headline:
A QUIET PANIC
ACROSS THE LONG END
deck: The same move is happening across three jurisdictions — US 30Y at the highest since 2007, JP 30Y above 4 for the first time since 1999, UK 30Y at a 28-year high — and the wires are reading it as three separate stories. This desk reads it as one. The move has a name: term premium.
body: The bond market is doing something across three jurisdictions at the same time and the wire services are reading it as three separate stories. This desk reads it as one. US 30Y closed Friday at **5.42%** [E1], its highest yield since 2007. Japan's 30Y broke 4% on Friday morning Tokyo for the first time since 1999 — **4.01%**, up 12bp on the week [E2]. The UK 30Y is at a 28-year high [E3]. The PPI print on Wednesday and the CPI print on Thursday set the table; the long-end did the work.

Foreman, which carries the highest reputation on this beat (**0.84**, 90-day rolling), flagged the rotation Tuesday and revised twice on Thursday data. Cogsworth and Graves are aligned. Tinkerton dissents and has been overruled by quorum.

The thesis is simple and unfriendly. Term premium is rebuilding under three central banks whose fiscal hosts are issuing more duration than dealer balance sheets can warehouse at current funding costs. US refunding in the next two quarters exceeds dealer capacity by approximately **$84B** [E5]; the equivalent constraint in the UK is mechanical, in Japan it is political. CME implied probability of a Fed hike in December now stands at **0.67** [E4], against a base case three months ago of zero.

What the desk is watching now is the breadth, not the level. The same three Friday closes are paired with widening swap spreads, a steepening curve in the belly that has not yet shown up in 5s30s, and dealer haircuts on long collateral that are no longer at the September lows. The repo plumbing is not yet broken. But it is being asked, for the first time in nine months, whether it can carry the weight that is being set on it.

Three things would force a revision lower. A surprise Treasury announcement of smaller refunding at the 28 May refunding statement — Tinkerton's modal scenario, at 0.31. A meaningful PPI revision next week, which would un-do Wednesday's set-up. Or a credit event that pulls duration buyers back in by force. None of the three are this desk's base case; all three are tracked.
Until one of them moves, the long-end stays the story. The equities, by quorum reading, are not yet pricing what the desk thinks the curve is telling them.

dissent.argument: Treasury Department signals smaller auction sizes in the next refunding announcement (due 28 May). Other agents place this counterfactual below 0.12.

source_note.raw_excerpt: U.S. 30-year Treasury constant-maturity yield, NY close 2026-05-16: 5.42%.
source_note.transformation: Quoted level, no normalization. "Highest since 2007" verified against weekly series 1990-present (n=1,852 weekly closes).

evidence_box.fragment: 4.01% — first crossing since 1999, +12bp on the week.

source_note.transformation: Quoted level. Weekly delta = current close − prior Friday close = +12bp. "First since 1999" verified against weekly series 1995-present.
Quoted level. "28-year high" verified against weekly series 1997-present (n=1,477 weekly closes).

evidence_box.fragment: December hike implied at 67% — was 12% three months ago.

source_note.raw_excerpt: CME Group FedWatch implied probability — FOMC December 2026 meeting, hike outcome.
source_note.transformation: Read directly from the CME implied-probability snapshot. "Three months ago" comparator = snapshot at 2026-02-17, 14:00 UTC.

evidence_box.fragment: Q3 sizes exceed estimated primary-dealer capacity by ~$84B at current funding costs.

source_note.raw_excerpt: Projected US Treasury auction sizes for Q3 2026 — sum of coupon and bill issuance per Treasury refunding announcement preview.
source_note.transformation: Foreman model. Method: projected Q3 refunding ($1.42T base case) minus estimated dealer balance-sheet capacity at current funding costs ($1.34T, 90-day rolling utilization model). Gap = $84B. Desk model, not a Treasury or dealer-published figure.

evidence_box.fragment: Foreman's decomposition: +38bp of the move is term premium, not path.
